questions.question: What does Java's default (no-keyword) access modifier mean, and what is its closest C# analogue?
choices: Package-private (visible within the same package only); roughly analogous to C# internal but scoped per-package rather than per-assembly.
questions.explanation: The default access in Java is package-private: visible to classes in the same package, invisible outside. C# has no per-package equivalent; the closest is internal, which is per-assembly. JPMS modules add a higher level but app code rarely adopts them.

questions.question: Your test in src/test/java/com/example/orders/OrderMapperTest.java needs to call a package-private OrderMapper. What's the standard approach in Java?
choices: Use reflection to bypass the access check.
Place the test in the same package (com.example.orders) under src/test/java; package-private is visible because both source trees are merged into one logical package.
Make the method public; you cannot test package-private code otherwise.
questions.explanation: Tests under src/test/java mirror the main source's package structure; the compiler treats them as the same package, so package-private members are visible. This is Java's standard answer to C#'s InternalsVisibleTo and works without any special declarations.

questions.question: Should a typical Spring Boot service adopt JPMS (Java Platform Module System)?
choices: Yes - JPMS is mandatory for Spring Boot 3.
No - JPMS adds friction (especially around reflection-heavy frameworks needing 'opens'), and most app code gets the boundaries it needs from packages plus tooling like ArchUnit.
Yes - JPMS replaces Maven for dependency management.
Only on Java 21; JPMS isn't available on Java 17.
questions.explanation: JPMS is optional for application code and uncommon in Spring Boot services. The JDK itself is modular, but adopting JPMS in a Spring app means declaring 'opens' for Jackson, Hibernate, and Spring reflection. Packages + ArchUnit-style rules cover the same need without the friction.
